firmware.name: grbl
firmware.description: Open-source CNC motion controller for Arduino. Based on the NIST RS274/NGC standard with adaptations for resource-constrained microcontrollers.

codes.name: Rapid Move
codes.description: Moves to target coordinates at maximum traverse rate (seek rate). No feed rate required. Each axis moves at its own maximum rate, so the path is not guaranteed to be a straight line. Subject to rapid overrides (100%/50%/25%).

examples.description: Rapid move to origin at Z safe height

crossReferences.note: Identical behavior per NIST standard. LinuxCNC may blend rapids with G64 path blending; grbl does not support path blending.
Smoothieware treats G0 identically but uses separately configurable per-axis max rates.
In RepRapFirmware, G0 and G1 are identical — both use the F parameter. In grbl, G0 always moves at maximum rate regardless of F.

modeNotes.description: G0 moves automatically disable the laser (S value is preserved but PWM output is 0). This prevents burning during positioning moves.

codes.notes: G0 is modal — once set, subsequent lines without a G command will continue to use G0. Rapids may not follow a straight line because each axis moves at its own max rate independently. Rapid overrides (50%/25%) are a grbl extension not found in NIST standard.

codes.name: Linear Move
codes.description: Controlled straight-line move at the specified feed rate. The tool path is always a precise straight line with all axes arriving simultaneously. Subject to feed rate overrides (10%-200%).

examples.description: Linear move to X50 Y30 at 500 units/min
Plunge cut to Z-1 at 100 units/min

crossReferences.note: Identical behavior. LinuxCNC supports additional axes (A, B, C, U, V, W) not available in grbl.
RepRapFirmware uses the S parameter on G1 for laser power (since RRF 2.02+). In grbl, laser power is always set with S on M3/M4, not on G1.

modeNotes.description: Laser is active during G1 moves at the power level set by M3/M4 S value. With M4 dynamic mode, power scales proportionally with speed during acceleration/deceleration. Inline S value changes update PWM instantaneously without stopping motion.

codes.notes: G1 is modal. The F parameter is also modal — once set, it persists until changed.

codes.name: Clockwise Arc
codes.description: Clockwise circular or helical interpolation in the currently selected plane (G17/G18/G19). Internally rendered as tiny line segments controlled by $12 (arc tolerance).

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: Arc center offset in X (incremental from current position)

parameters.name: J
parameters.description: Arc center offset in Y (incremental from current position)

parameters.name: K
parameters.description: Arc center offset in Z (incremental from current position)

examples.description: CW arc to X10 Y10 with center offset I5 J0
CW arc to X10 Y10 with radius 5 (shorter arc)

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. LinuxCNC also supports absolute IJK mode (G90.1); grbl only supports incremental IJK (G91.1).

modeNotes.description: Laser is active during arcs. With M4, dynamic power scales with instantaneous speed.

codes.notes: Only incremental IJK mode (G91.1) is supported — arc center offsets are always relative to the current position. Absolute IJK (G90.1) is NOT supported. Use either IJK offsets or R radius, not both.

codes.name: Counter-Clockwise Arc
codes.description: Counter-clockwise circular or helical interpolation. Same as G2 but in the opposite direction.

examples.description: CCW arc to X10 Y10 with radius 5

crossReferences.note: Same behavior, same IJK limitation as G2.

codes.name: Dwell
codes.description: Pauses execution for a specified duration. Ignored in check mode ($C). Non-modal.

crossReferences.note: LinuxCNC also supports Q parameter for seconds. Some implementations interpret P as milliseconds — grbl uses seconds.

modeNotes.description: With M3 (constant), laser stays on during dwell. With M4 (dynamic), laser turns off during dwell since speed is zero.

codes.notes: P is in seconds (not milliseconds). This can differ from some other controllers.

codes.name: Set Coordinate System Data
codes.description: Sets work coordinate system offsets. Always used with an L word to specify the operation: L2 sets absolute offsets, L20 sets offsets relative to current position. Defined by the NIST RS274/NGC standard.

parameters.name: L
parameters.description: Operation type: L2 = set absolute offset, L20 = set offset relative to current position

examples.code: G10 L2 P1 X10 Y20 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 offset to absolute values X10 Y20 Z0

examples.code: G10 L20 P1 X0 Y0 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 so current position becomes X0 Y0 Z0

crossReferences.note: grblHAL extends G10 with additional L words (L1, L10, L11) for tool offsets and supports P7-P9 for G59.1-G59.3.
LinuxCNC supports the same L2/L20 operations plus L1/L10/L11 for tool offsets and the R parameter for coordinate system rotation.
RepRapFirmware overloads G10: without parameters it performs firmware retract; with L/P parameters it sets offsets like grbl.
Smoothieware uses bare G10 for firmware retract only. Coordinate offsets use G10 L2/L20 as separate commands.

codes.notes: grbl supports L2 and L20 only. Offsets are stored persistently in EEPROM. Do not place in $N startup blocks as this causes an EEPROM write on every startup. Supports 6 coordinate systems (P1-P6, mapping to G54-G59).

codes.name: Set Work Coordinate Offset (Absolute)
codes.description: Sets a work coordinate system offset (G54-G59) to absolute values. The offset is stored persistently in EEPROM.

examples.code: G10 L2 P1 X10 Y20 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 offset to X10 Y20 Z0

codes.notes: Persistent in EEPROM. Do not place in $N startup blocks (EEPROM write on every startup).

codes.name: Set Work Coordinate Offset (Relative)
codes.description: Sets a work coordinate system offset such that the current position becomes the specified coordinate value. Persistent in EEPROM.

parameters.name: X
parameters.description: Desired X position at current location

parameters.name: Y
parameters.description: Desired Y position at current location

parameters.name: Z
parameters.description: Desired Z position at current location

examples.code: G10 L20 P1 X0 Y0 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 so current position reads as X0 Y0 Z0

codes.notes: Commonly used for setting work zero at the current position. Do not place in $N startup blocks.

codes.name: XY Plane Select
codes.description: Selects XY as the active plane for arc commands (G2/G3). I and J specify arc center offsets. Default plane.

crossReferences.note: Identical. LinuxCNC also supports G17.1 (UW plane) not available in grbl.

codes.name: ZX Plane Select
codes.description: Selects ZX as the active plane for arc commands. I and K specify arc center offsets.

codes.name: YZ Plane Select
codes.description: Selects YZ as the active plane for arc commands. J and K specify arc center offsets.

codes.name: Inch Mode
codes.description: Interprets all coordinate and feed rate values in inches.

examples.description: Move to X=1 inch at 10 inches/min

codes.name: Millimeter Mode
codes.description: Interprets all coordinate and feed rate values in millimeters. This is the default.

codes.name: Go to Predefined Position 1
codes.description: Moves to the predefined home position stored in EEPROM (set by G28.1). If axis words are given, moves to those coordinates first as an intermediate point, then to the stored position. This is NOT homing — use $H for homing.

examples.description: Move directly to stored G28 position
Move Z to 5 first, then go to stored G28 position

crossReferences.note: WARNING: In Smoothieware, G28 performs a homing cycle (equivalent to grbl's $H), NOT a move to a stored position. This is a critical difference that can cause crashes.
WARNING: In RepRapFirmware, G28 also performs a homing cycle via macro files, not a move to a stored position.

modeNotes.description: Treated as a rapid move — laser is disabled during G28 motion.

codes.notes: CRITICAL: G28 is NOT homing. In grbl, homing is $H. In 3D printing firmwares (Smoothieware, RepRapFirmware), G28 IS the homing command. This difference can cause machine crashes when sharing G-code between CNC and 3D printing firmware.

codes.name: Set Predefined Position 1
codes.description: Stores the current machine position as the G28 target position. Persistent in EEPROM.

examples.description: Save current position as G28 target

codes.notes: Do not place in $N startup blocks — causes an EEPROM write on every startup.

codes.name: Go to Predefined Position 2
codes.description: Same as G28 but uses a second stored position (set by G30.1).

crossReferences.note: In Smoothieware, G30 is a single-point probe command, NOT a move to a stored position.

codes.name: Set Predefined Position 2
codes.description: Stores the current machine position as the G30 target position. Persistent in EEPROM.

examples.description: Save current position as G30 target

codes.notes: Do not place in $N startup blocks.

codes.name: Probe Toward Workpiece (Error on No Contact)
codes.description: Linear probe move toward workpiece. If the probe does not make contact before reaching the target position, an alarm is thrown. Probe result stored in PRB parameter (viewable via $#).

examples.description: Probe downward to Z-10 at 50mm/min; alarm if no contact

modeNotes.description: Laser is automatically disabled during probe cycles.

codes.notes: PRB result includes success flag (:1 or :0). Non-persistent — cleared on reset.

codes.name: Probe Toward Workpiece (No Error)
codes.description: Same as G38.2 but does not alarm on failure. Sets PRB flag to :0 if no contact made.

examples.description: Probe downward; no alarm if contact missed

codes.name: Probe Away from Workpiece (Error on No Contact)
codes.description: Probe expects to start in contact and moves away. Alarm if still in contact at target position.

examples.description: Probe upward away from surface; alarm if still in contact

codes.name: Probe Away from Workpiece (No Error)
codes.description: Same as G38.4 but does not alarm on failure.

examples.description: Probe away from surface; no alarm if still in contact

codes.name: Cutter Compensation Off
codes.description: Disables cutter radius compensation. This is the default and only supported mode — grbl does NOT support G41/G42 cutter compensation.

crossReferences.note: LinuxCNC supports full cutter compensation (G40/G41/G42). grbl only supports G40 (off).

codes.notes: G41/G42 cutter radius compensation is NOT implemented in grbl. CAM software must handle tool offset compensation.

codes.name: Dynamic Tool Length Offset
codes.description: Applies a tool length offset. Only the dynamic form (G43.1) is supported — grbl has no tool table. Non-persistent (cleared on reset). Only applies to the configured tool axis (typically Z).

crossReferences.note: LinuxCNC supports both G43 (from tool table) and G43.1 (dynamic). grbl only supports G43.1.
grblHAL supports both G43 (with tool table) and G43.1. grbl only supports G43.1.

codes.notes: Only G43.1 (dynamic) is supported, not G43 (from tool table). Returns error:37 if applied to wrong axis.

codes.name: Cancel Tool Length Offset
codes.description: Cancels any active tool length offset. Default state.

codes.name: Move in Machine Coordinates
codes.description: Overrides the active work coordinate system for the current line only. Must be paired with G0 or G1. Non-modal — only applies to the line it's on.

examples.description: Rapid Z to machine zero (top of travel)

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. Must be on same line as G0 or G1.

codes.notes: Returns error:30 if not paired with G0 or G1. Also accepted in $J jog commands (grbl extension).

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 1
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 1. This is the default coordinate system. Offset is stored in EEPROM and set via G10 L2/L20.

crossReferences.note: Identical. LinuxCNC also supports G59.1/G59.2/G59.3 (9 total systems); grbl only has 6.

codes.notes: grbl supports G54-G59 (6 work coordinate systems). G59.1/G59.2/G59.3 are NOT supported (error:29). View offsets with $#.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 2
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 2.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 3
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 3.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 4
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 4.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 5
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 5.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 6
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 6.

codes.name: Exact Stop Mode
codes.description: Machine decelerates to a complete stop at the end of each motion command before executing the next. Default path control mode in grbl.

crossReferences.note: LinuxCNC also supports G61.1 (exact path) and G64 (path blending with tolerance). grbl only supports G61.

codes.notes: G64 path blending is NOT supported. grbl handles cornering via the $11 junction deviation setting instead.

codes.name: Motion Mode Cancel
codes.description: Cancels the active motion mode. No axis words should be present when G80 is active.
codes.notes: Returns error:31 if axis words are given while G80 is active.

codes.name: Absolute Distance Mode
codes.description: All subsequent movement coordinates are interpreted as absolute positions relative to the current work coordinate system origin (set by G54-G59). Default mode.

examples.description: Moves to absolute positions: first to (10,10), then to (20,20)

crossReferences.note: Same for XYZ. Smoothieware also supports M82/M83 for independent extruder positioning mode.

codes.notes: Default mode. Coordinates are relative to the active work coordinate system, not machine zero. Use G53 for machine coordinate moves.

codes.name: Incremental Distance Mode
codes.description: All subsequent movement coordinates are interpreted as incremental distances from the current position.

examples.description: Moves incrementally: first +10 in X/Y, then another +10. Final position is (20,20) from start.

codes.notes: Be cautious with G91 — if a program is interrupted and restarted without resetting to G90, moves will be relative to the interrupted position.

codes.name: Incremental Arc Distance Mode
codes.description: Arc center offsets (I, J, K) are always relative to the current position. This is the default and ONLY arc offset mode grbl supports.

crossReferences.note: LinuxCNC also supports G90.1 (absolute arc offsets). grbl does not.

codes.notes: G90.1 (absolute arc center mode) is NOT supported in grbl. IJK offsets are always incremental.

codes.name: Set Coordinate System Offset
codes.description: Sets a temporary coordinate offset so that the current position reads as the specified value. Non-persistent — cleared on reset.

parameters.name: X
parameters.description: Desired X position at current location

parameters.name: Y
parameters.description: Desired Y position at current location

parameters.name: Z
parameters.description: Desired Z position at current location

examples.description: Set current position as X0 Y0 temporarily

crossReferences.note: Same behavior but LinuxCNC can optionally persist G92 offsets (configurable).

codes.notes: Non-persistent in grbl (cleared on reset). Prefer G10 L20 for persistent work offsets. G92 applies on top of the active work coordinate system.

codes.name: Clear G92 Offset
codes.description: Cancels the G92 offset, resetting it to zero.

codes.name: Inverse Time Feed Rate Mode
codes.description: Feed rate F specifies the inverse of time in minutes to complete the move. Each motion command MUST include an F word. F2 means the move should complete in 0.5 minutes (1/2).
codes.notes: Every motion line must include F in inverse time mode. Commonly used with 4th/5th axis work or complex tool paths.

codes.name: Units Per Minute Feed Rate Mode
codes.description: Feed rate F is in units (mm or inches) per minute. This is the default mode. F is modal — persists until changed.

codes.name: Program Pause
codes.description: Halts program execution. Resume with cycle start (~). Spindle and coolant remain in current state.

examples.description: Pause program — wait for operator to resume

codes.name: Optional Pause
codes.description: Conditional program pause. In grbl, M1 always pauses (same as M0) because there is no optional stop switch.

crossReferences.note: In LinuxCNC, M1 only pauses if the optional stop switch is active. In grbl, it always pauses.

codes.notes: Always pauses in grbl — no optional stop switch support.

codes.name: Program End
codes.description: Ends the program. Stops spindle and coolant. Does not rewind.

codes.name: Spindle On (CW) / Laser On (Constant)
codes.description: Turns the spindle on clockwise at the speed specified by S. In laser mode, enables constant power mode.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: Spindle speed (RPM) or laser power (0 to $30 max)

examples.description: Laser mode: constant power cut to X10

crossReferences.note: Identical for spindle control. LinuxCNC does not have a built-in laser mode.
Same behavior. grblHAL extends S value range and adds spindle sync features.
Smoothieware does not differentiate M3/M4 for laser mode — laser behavior is module-configured.
In RRF, the mode (FDM/Laser/CNC) is set by M451/M452/M453. Laser power can also be set per-move via S on G1.

modeNotes.description: Controls spindle direction and speed. grbl waits for spindle to reach speed before motion. Spindle remains on during G0 moves.
Constant power mode — laser fires at the S power level during ALL moves including G0. Power stays on even when stationary. Use M4 instead for safe laser operation in most cases.

codes.notes: In laser mode, M3 keeps the laser on even during G0 rapids and when stationary. This is usually NOT what you want — use M4 for dynamic power mode which automatically disables the laser during rapids and scales with speed.

codes.name: Spindle On (CCW) / Laser On (Dynamic)
codes.description: Turns the spindle on counter-clockwise. In laser mode, enables dynamic power mode where laser power scales proportionally with speed and disables during rapids.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: Spindle speed (RPM) or laser power (0 to $30 max)

examples.description: Laser mode: dynamic power cut to X50

crossReferences.note: In LinuxCNC, M4 is only CCW spindle rotation. No laser mode behavior.
Smoothieware does not differentiate M3/M4 for laser mode. No dynamic power scaling equivalent.

modeNotes.description: Counter-clockwise spindle rotation at the specified S speed.

Dynamic power mode. Power scales proportionally to current speed vs. programmed feed rate. Laser off when stationary, off during G0, reduced during acceleration/deceleration. Prevents burn marks at corners and start/stop points.

codes.notes: M4 dynamic mode is the preferred and recommended mode for most laser operations. It produces consistent energy delivery along the cut path and is safer than M3 because the laser turns off when not moving.

codes.name: Spindle/Laser Off
codes.description: Turns the spindle or laser off. In laser mode, S0 also disables the laser instantaneously without stopping motion.
codes.notes: Default state. In laser mode, inline S0 on a motion command also disables laser without halting the motion planner.

codes.name: Mist Coolant On
codes.description: Enables mist coolant output. Requires ENABLE_M7 compile-time option to be enabled in grbl config.h.

crossReferences.note: Always available in grblHAL (no compile-time option needed).

codes.notes: Requires compile-time ENABLE_M7 option. Can be toggled in real-time via 0xA1 command.

codes.name: Flood Coolant On
codes.description: Enables flood coolant output.
codes.notes: Can be toggled in real-time via 0xA0 command.

codes.name: All Coolant Off
codes.description: Disables both mist and flood coolant. Default state.

codes.name: Program End and Rewind
codes.description: Ends the program, stops spindle and coolant, and rewinds to beginning. Triggers a feedback message.

codes.name: Run Homing Cycle
codes.description: Initiates the homing sequence — moves each axis toward its limit switches to establish machine zero. Only available when $22=1 (homing enabled). Searches at $25 seek rate, re-approaches at $24 feed rate, then pulls off by $27 distance.

crossReferences.note: grblHAL adds single-axis homing ($HX, $HY, $HZ) and auto-squaring for ganged axes. grbl only homes all axes at once.
Smoothieware uses G28 for homing. No $ command equivalent.
RepRapFirmware uses G28 for homing with configurable macro files.
LinuxCNC handles homing through the GUI/HAL interface, not G-code commands.

codes.notes: grbl locks out all G-code until homing is complete when $22=1. Homing order and direction are configurable via $23. Pull-off distance via $27. grbl only supports homing all axes at once — single-axis homing requires grblHAL.

codes.name: Jog Command
codes.description: Executes a jogging motion that can be cancelled in real-time. Unlike G0/G1, jog commands do not affect the G-code parser state and can be interrupted instantly. Introduced in grbl 1.1.

parameters.name: F
parameters.description: Jog feed rate (REQUIRED, always units/min regardless of G93)

parameters.name: G90/G91
parameters.description: Distance mode override for this jog only

parameters.name: G20/G21
parameters.description: Units override for this jog only

examples.description: Jog to Y5 in machine coordinates

crossReferences.note: Smoothieware has no dedicated jog command. Jogging is done with G0 moves.
LinuxCNC handles jogging through the UI/HAL interface, not G-code commands.

codes.notes: The = sign after $J is required. Cancel with jog-cancel (0x85), feed hold (!), or safety door. Does NOT alter modal state. S and T words are NOT accepted. Soft-limit violations return errors instead of alarms. Send G4P0 after jog cancel to synchronize.

codes.name: Kill Alarm Lock
codes.description: Overrides the alarm lock to allow movement. Use carefully — machine position may be lost after alarm. Recommended to use G91 incremental mode after unlocking.
codes.notes: Does not execute startup blocks. Use for emergency recovery only — position may be unknown after alarm.

codes.name: Check G-Code Mode
codes.description: Toggles check mode on/off. In check mode, grbl parses G-code and reports errors without moving any axes. Dwells are ignored, spindle and coolant are disabled. Auto-resets when toggled off.
codes.notes: Useful for validating G-code files before running them. Machine performs a soft-reset when exiting check mode.

codes.name: View Settings
codes.description: Displays all current grbl settings ($0 through $132) stored in EEPROM.

crossReferences.note: grblHAL has additional settings beyond $132 for extra axes, networking, tool change, etc.

codes.notes: Settings include step configuration ($0-$6), motion parameters ($11-$12), limits/homing ($20-$27), spindle ($30-$32), steps/mm ($100-$102), max rates ($110-$112), acceleration ($120-$122), and max travel ($130-$132).

codes.name: View G-Code Parameters
codes.description: Displays G54-G59 work coordinate offsets, G28/G30 stored positions, G92 offset, tool length offset (TLO), and last probe result (PRB) with success flag.

codes.name: View Parser State
codes.description: Returns the current active modal states including motion mode, coordinate system, plane, units, distance mode, feed rate mode, tool, spindle speed, and feed rate.

examples.description: Returns [GC:G0 G54 G17 G21 G90 G94 M5 M9 T0 S0 F0]

codes.name: View Build Info
codes.description: Returns grbl version, build date, and optional custom user string. User string can be set with $I=xxx (max 80 chars).

codes.name: View Startup Blocks
codes.description: Shows the two startup G-code lines ($N0 and $N1) that execute on every power-up or reset after homing (if enabled).

examples.description: Set first startup line to select G54 and mm mode

codes.notes: Never place EEPROM-writing commands (G10, G28.1, G30.1) in startup blocks. Startup blocks do NOT execute after $X unlock.

codes.name: Sleep Mode
codes.description: De-powers spindle, coolant, and stepper enable pins. Blocks all commands. Can only exit via soft-reset (Ctrl-X) or power cycle. Returns to ALARM state on re-init.
codes.notes: grbl extension. Not part of any G-code standard.

codes.name: Restore Default Settings
codes.description: Restores all $0-$132 settings to their compile-time default values.

codes.name: Clear Coordinate Data
codes.description: Clears all G54-G59 work coordinate offsets and G28/G30 stored positions from EEPROM.

codes.name: Full EEPROM Wipe
codes.description: Clears ALL EEPROM data: settings, coordinate parameters, startup lines, and build info string.

codes.name: Step Pulse Time
codes.description: Sets the length of each step pulse in microseconds. This is the duration the step pin is held HIGH for each motor step. Most stepper drivers need at least 2-3us, but noisy or optocoupled setups may need more. Increase if you experience missed steps or erratic motion.

examples.description: Set step pulse to 5us for faster drivers

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 10us. Valid range: 3-255us. Most modern stepper drivers (DRV8825, TMC2209) work fine at 3-5us. Increase to 10+ if using optocoupled breakout boards.

codes.name: Step Idle Delay
codes.description: Sets the delay in milliseconds before stepper motors are disabled after completing a motion. When set to 255, steppers are never disabled (always energized). Lower values save power and reduce heat but allow the machine to drift out of position when idle.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. grblHAL also adds $37 (stepper deenergize mask) for per-axis control.

codes.notes: Default: 25ms. Set to 255 for machines where losing position is unacceptable (e.g., vertical Z axis that drops under gravity). NEMA17 motors may get warm when always energized — that is normal.

codes.name: Step Port Invert Mask
codes.description: Bitmask that inverts the step signal polarity for each axis. Bit 0=X, Bit 1=Y, Bit 2=Z. Use when a stepper driver expects active-low step signals instead of the default active-high.

examples.description: No inversion (default, all step signals active-high)

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0. Bitmask values: X=1, Y=2, Z=4. Combine by adding (e.g., X+Z = 1+4 = 5). Rarely needs changing — most drivers expect active-high.

codes.name: Direction Port Invert Mask
codes.description: Bitmask that inverts the direction signal for each axis. Use to reverse the positive direction of an axis without rewiring the motor. Bit 0=X, Bit 1=Y, Bit 2=Z.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0. Most common setting to change during initial machine setup. If an axis moves the wrong way, flip its bit here. Bitmask: X=1, Y=2, Z=4.

codes.name: Step Enable Invert
codes.description: Inverts the stepper enable pin logic. When disabled (0), the enable pin is LOW to enable drivers. Set to 1 if your drivers require HIGH to enable.

examples.description: Normal enable logic — LOW enables drivers (default)

crossReferences.note: Modified in grblHAL: changed from a single boolean to a per-axis bitmask. In grbl, $4 is a simple 0/1 toggle for all axes.

codes.notes: Default: 0 (LOW=enabled). Boolean in grbl. If your motors are locked when they should be free and free when they should be locked, toggle this setting.

codes.name: Limit Pins Invert
codes.description: Inverts the logic of the limit switch input pins. Set to 1 if using normally-closed (NC) limit switches, which is recommended for noise immunity.

examples.description: Normal logic — switches trigger on HIGH (default, for NO switches)
Inverted logic — switches trigger on LOW (for NC switches)

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0. Normally-closed (NC) switches are strongly recommended for CNC machines as they are more noise-resistant and fail-safe (a broken wire triggers the limit, alerting you to a problem).

codes.name: Probe Pin Invert
codes.description: Inverts the logic of the probe input pin. Use to match your probe's switch type (NO vs NC).

examples.description: Normal logic — probe triggers on HIGH (default)

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0. Set based on whether your touch plate or probe uses a normally-open (NO) or normally-closed (NC) switch.

codes.name: Status Report Mask
codes.description: Configures what data the '?' real-time status report includes. Controls whether work position (WPos) or machine position (MPos) is reported, and whether planner/serial buffer data is included.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. grblHAL may report additional fields.

codes.notes: Default: 1 (MPos). Bit 0: 0=WPos, 1=MPos. Bit 1: include buffer data (Buf:). Most UIs work with either WPos or MPos, as the other can be computed from the work coordinate offset. Buffer data is useful for debugging streaming.

codes.name: Junction Deviation
codes.description: Sets the junction deviation value in mm, which controls how fast the machine takes corners between line segments. Higher values allow faster cornering but less precise path following. Lower values produce more accurate corners but slower motion.

examples.description: Default — good balance of speed and accuracy
Tighter corners, slower (for fine detail work)

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0.010mm. This is an advanced setting. The junction deviation algorithm determines how much the machine slows down at corners. Most users should leave this at the default unless experiencing issues with corner quality.

codes.name: Arc Tolerance
codes.description: Sets the precision of arc rendering in mm. Arcs (G2/G3) are internally converted to tiny line segments; this value controls how closely those segments approximate the ideal arc. Smaller values produce smoother arcs but generate more segments.

examples.description: Default — good for most CNC work

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0.002mm. The arc tolerance is the maximum deviation between the ideal arc and the line segment approximation. Lower values produce smoother arcs but may slow down motion on resource-constrained hardware.

codes.name: Report Inches
codes.description: Switches status report position values between mm (0) and inches (1). This only affects the reporting format — internal calculations and G-code parsing remain the same and follow G20/G21.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0 (mm). Only affects the ? status report output. G-code parsing uses the current G20/G21 mode. Most users leave this at 0 (mm) since most sender software handles unit conversion.

codes.name: Soft Limits Enable
codes.description: Enables software-enforced travel limits. When enabled, grbl checks if a move would exceed the max travel ($130-$132) and rejects it with an error before motion starts. Requires homing ($22=1) to establish a reference position.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0. Requires $22=1 (homing enabled) and $130-$132 (max travel) properly set. Prevents the machine from crashing into its limits. Checks entire move path, including arcs. Does NOT protect jog commands ($J).

codes.name: Hard Limits Enable
codes.description: Enables hardware limit switches. When triggered during motion, grbl immediately halts all motion and enters an ALARM state. Strongly recommended for machine protection, but requires proper switch wiring and electrical noise filtering.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0. Uses interrupt-driven detection for instant response. WARNING: Electrical noise from stepper motors or VFDs can cause false triggers. Use shielded cables, NC switches, and add filtering capacitors if experiencing false alarms. Recovery from a hard limit alarm requires $X then re-homing.

codes.name: Homing Enable
codes.description: Enables the homing cycle ($H command). When enabled, grbl locks out all G-code commands at startup until the machine is homed. Required for soft limits ($20) and accurate machine coordinates.

crossReferences.note: Modified in grblHAL: changed from boolean to a bitmask with additional options — bit 0: enable homing, bit 1: enable single-axis homing, bit 2: force homing on startup, bit 3: manual homing via limit switches, etc.

codes.notes: Default: 0. When enabled, the machine enters alarm state on startup and requires $H before any motion. Use $X to bypass homing lock if needed (not recommended for production). Configure homing behavior with $23-$27.

codes.name: Homing Direction Invert Mask
codes.description: Bitmask that controls which direction each axis moves during the homing search. By default, all axes home toward the negative direction. Set a bit to home that axis toward the positive direction instead.

examples.description: All axes home in negative direction (default)
X homes positive, Y and Z home negative
X and Y home positive, Z homes negative

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 0. Bitmask: X=1, Y=2, Z=4. Most CNC machines home Z to positive (top) and X/Y to negative (front-left), so $23=4 is common. grbl always homes Z first, then X and Y simultaneously.

codes.name: Homing Locate Feed Rate
codes.description: Sets the slow feed rate in mm/min used during the second homing approach (locate phase). After the initial fast seek hits the switch, the axis backs off and re-approaches at this slower rate for precise home position determination.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 25.000 mm/min. This is the slow, precise second approach. Should be significantly slower than $25 (seek rate) for accuracy. Typical range: 10-100 mm/min.

codes.name: Homing Search Seek Rate
codes.description: Sets the fast seek rate in mm/min used during the first homing approach. The axis moves at this rate until it hits the limit switch, then backs off and re-approaches at the slower $24 rate.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 500.000 mm/min. This is the initial fast search rate. Set high enough for reasonable homing time but not so fast that the machine overshoots or stalls. Must be less than the axis max rate ($110-$112).

codes.name: Homing Debounce Delay
codes.description: Sets the debounce delay in milliseconds for homing limit switches. After a switch triggers, grbl waits this long for the signal to settle before confirming the switch state. Helps prevent false triggers from switch bounce.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 250ms. Mechanical switches may bounce for several milliseconds. Optical or hall-effect switches can use a much shorter debounce. If homing is unreliable, try increasing this value.

codes.name: Homing Pull-off Distance
codes.description: Sets the distance in mm that each axis backs off from the limit switch after homing completes. This ensures the switch is fully deactivated after homing, preventing immediate hard limit alarms during normal operation.

examples.description: Pull off 5mm (for switches with wide activation zones)

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 1.000mm. If you get an alarm immediately after homing completes, increase this value — the switch is not fully clearing. Must be large enough to completely deactivate the limit switch.

codes.name: Max Spindle Speed
codes.description: Sets the maximum spindle speed in RPM. This value is mapped to the maximum PWM duty cycle output. Used by the S command to scale spindle speed proportionally across the PWM range.

examples.description: Max 24000 RPM (typical for CNC router spindles)

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

modeNotes.description: In laser mode, $30 represents the maximum laser power. S values above $30 are clamped to this value.

codes.notes: Default: 1000. Set to match your spindle's actual max RPM. The S value in G-code is mapped: S_value / $30 = PWM duty cycle. For a VFD-controlled spindle, set to the VFD's max RPM and configure the VFD to accept 0-5V/0-10V from the PWM output.

codes.name: Min Spindle Speed
codes.description: Sets the minimum spindle speed in RPM. This value is mapped to the minimum PWM duty cycle. Any S value at or below this produces the minimum PWM output. Useful for spindles or lasers that have a minimum operating speed/power.

examples.description: Minimum 5000 RPM (for spindles with minimum operating speed)

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

modeNotes.description: In laser mode, $31 sets the minimum laser power threshold. S values below this will produce the minimum PWM output, not zero.

codes.notes: Default: 0. Set this if your spindle or laser requires a minimum PWM duty cycle to operate. S0 with M3/M4 still outputs zero PWM (spindle off is always off).

codes.name: Laser Mode
codes.description: Enables laser mode, which changes how the spindle PWM output behaves during motion. In laser mode, the laser automatically turns off during G0 rapids, and M4 enables dynamic power scaling that adjusts laser power proportionally with speed during acceleration/deceleration.

crossReferences.note: Extended in grblHAL: 0=normal spindle, 1=laser mode, 2=lathe mode. grbl only supports 0 and 1.

modeNotes.description: G0 rapids disable laser output. M4 enables dynamic power scaling (power proportional to speed during accel/decel). Inline S value changes on G1/G2/G3 update PWM without stopping motion. G1 F0 or dwelling with laser on will produce constant output.

codes.notes: Default: 0. CRITICAL SAFETY: Only enable laser mode with an actual laser. In laser mode, M4 dynamic power mode prevents burning during acceleration/deceleration by scaling power with feed rate. Use M3 for constant power (e.g., cutting), M4 for dynamic power (e.g., engraving).

codes.name: X Steps/mm
codes.description: Sets the number of step pulses required to move the X axis one millimeter. This is the fundamental calibration setting for X axis positioning accuracy. Calculated from motor steps/rev, microstepping, and drive mechanism (leadscrew pitch, belt/pulley, etc.).

examples.description: 800 steps/mm (e.g., 200 step motor, 1/16 microstep, 4mm pitch leadscrew)

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior. grblHAL extends with $103-$109 for axes A through W.

codes.notes: Default: 250.000. Formula for leadscrews: (motor_steps * microstepping) / screw_pitch_mm. Formula for belts: (motor_steps * microstepping) / (tooth_count * belt_pitch_mm). Verify by commanding a known distance and measuring actual travel.

codes.name: Y Steps/mm
codes.description: Sets the number of step pulses required to move the Y axis one millimeter. Same calculation as $100 but for the Y axis.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 250.000. See $100 for calculation details.

codes.name: Z Steps/mm
codes.description: Sets the number of step pulses required to move the Z axis one millimeter. Same calculation as $100 but for the Z axis. Z often uses a different drive mechanism (e.g., ballscrew vs belt) so this value frequently differs from $100/$101.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 250.000. See $100 for calculation details.

codes.name: X Max Rate
codes.description: Sets the maximum travel rate for the X axis in mm/min. grbl will never exceed this speed on the X axis regardless of the programmed feed rate. Set conservatively — if set too high, the stepper will stall and lose steps.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior. grblHAL extends with $113-$119 for axes A through W.

codes.notes: Default: 500.000 mm/min. Start low and gradually increase until the motor stalls, then back off 10-20%. This limits G0 rapid speed and caps G1 feed rate for this axis. Also limits $25 homing seek rate.

codes.name: Y Max Rate
codes.description: Sets the maximum travel rate for the Y axis in mm/min. Same as $110 but for the Y axis.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 500.000 mm/min. See $110 for tuning guidance.

codes.name: Z Max Rate
codes.description: Sets the maximum travel rate for the Z axis in mm/min. Z axis is often slower than X/Y due to gravity loading and the drive mechanism used.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 500.000 mm/min. See $110 for tuning guidance. Z is often set lower than X/Y due to gravity and the mass of the spindle.

codes.name: X Acceleration
codes.description: Sets the acceleration for the X axis in mm/sec^2. Controls how quickly the axis ramps up to and down from the target speed. Higher values produce faster motion but risk losing steps if the motor cannot keep up.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior. grblHAL extends with $123-$129 for axes A through W.

codes.notes: Default: 10.000 mm/sec^2. Start low and increase gradually. Too high causes stalling and lost steps. Too low makes the machine sluggish and increases cycle time. Typical CNC router values: 50-500 mm/sec^2 depending on machine mass and motor torque.

codes.name: Y Acceleration
codes.description: Sets the acceleration for the Y axis in mm/sec^2. Same as $120 but for the Y axis.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 10.000 mm/sec^2. See $120 for tuning guidance.

codes.name: Z Acceleration
codes.description: Sets the acceleration for the Z axis in mm/sec^2. Same as $120 but for the Z axis. Z often needs lower acceleration due to gravity and spindle mass.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 10.000 mm/sec^2. See $120 for tuning guidance. Z acceleration is often set lower than X/Y.

codes.name: X Max Travel
codes.description: Sets the maximum travel distance for the X axis in mm, measured from the home position. Used by soft limits ($20) to prevent the machine from moving beyond its physical range. Always specified as a positive value.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior. grblHAL extends with $133-$139 for axes A through W.

codes.notes: Default: 200.000mm. Must be set accurately for soft limits ($20) to work. Measure the actual travel of your machine. Value is always positive regardless of homing direction.

codes.name: Y Max Travel
codes.description: Sets the maximum travel distance for the Y axis in mm. Same as $130 but for the Y axis.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 200.000mm. See $130 for details.

codes.name: Z Max Travel
codes.description: Sets the maximum travel distance for the Z axis in mm. Same as $130 but for the Z axis.

crossReferences.note: Same setting in grblHAL. Identical behavior.

codes.notes: Default: 200.000mm. See $130 for details.

codes.name: Soft Reset (Ctrl+X)
codes.description: Immediately halts and safely resets grbl without a power cycle. If reset while in motion, throws an alarm indicating position may be lost. If reset while not in motion, position is retained and re-homing is not required.
codes.notes: This is a real-time command — sent as a single byte (0x18), not as a G-code line. It is processed immediately regardless of buffer state.

codes.name: Status Report Query
codes.description: Immediately generates and sends back runtime data with a status report including machine state, position, feed rate, and buffer state. Accepted at any time except during homing and critical alarms.

crossReferences.note: Identical behavior. grblHAL extends the status report with additional fields.

codes.notes: Real-time command. Response format depends on $10 setting: MPos vs WPos, with or without buffer data. Format: <State|MPos:x,y,z|Bf:n,n|FS:n,n|Ov:n,n,n>

codes.name: Feed Hold
codes.description: Places grbl into a suspend/HOLD state. If in motion, the machine decelerates to a controlled stop and then suspends. Does not disable spindle or coolant — only halts motion. If jogging, cancels the jog and flushes remaining jog commands.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Executes in IDLE, RUN, or JOG states; ignored otherwise. Spindle and coolant remain active during hold.

codes.name: Cycle Start / Resume
codes.description: Resumes from a feed hold, safety door/parking state (when door is closed), or M0 program pause. If parking is enabled and safety door state is ready to resume, it re-enables the spindle and coolant, moves back into position, then resumes.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Only effective when in HOLD, DOOR, or paused states.

codes.name: Safety Door
codes.description: Immediately suspends into DOOR state. If in motion, decelerates to a stop then suspends. Disables spindle and coolant. If parking compile-time option is enabled, retracts the spindle to a safe position.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Executes in IDLE, HOLD, RUN, HOMING, or JOG states. More aggressive than feed hold — also disables spindle/coolant.

codes.name: Jog Cancel
codes.description: Immediately cancels the current jog state by performing a feed hold and automatically flushing any remaining jog commands in the buffer. Ignored if not in a JOG state.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Only active during JOG state — ignored in all other states.

codes.name: Feed Override Reset (100%)
codes.description: Resets the feed rate override to 100% of the programmed feed rate.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Feed overrides range from 10% to 200%. Does not affect rapid rates or jog motions.

codes.name: Feed Override +10%
codes.description: Increases the feed rate override by 10% of programmed feed rate.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Maximum feed override is 200%.

codes.name: Feed Override -10%
codes.description: Decreases the feed rate override by 10% of programmed feed rate.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Minimum feed override is 10%.

codes.name: Feed Override +1%
codes.description: Increases the feed rate override by 1% of programmed feed rate for fine adjustment.

codes.name: Feed Override -1%
codes.description: Decreases the feed rate override by 1% of programmed feed rate for fine adjustment.

codes.name: Rapid Override 100%
codes.description: Resets the rapid rate override to 100% full rapid rate.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Only affects G0, G28, and G30 rapid motions.

codes.name: Rapid Override 50%
codes.description: Sets the rapid rate override to 50% of full rapid rate.

codes.name: Rapid Override 25%
codes.description: Sets the rapid rate override to 25% of full rapid rate.

codes.name: Spindle Override Reset (100%)
codes.description: Resets the spindle speed override to 100% of programmed speed.

modeNotes.description: Spindle overrides also affect laser power. Use with caution — overriding laser power can affect cut quality or cause material damage.

codes.notes: Real-time command. Spindle overrides range from 10% to 200%. May be changed regardless of whether spindle is enabled.

codes.name: Spindle Override +10%
codes.description: Increases spindle speed override by 10%.

codes.name: Spindle Override -10%
codes.description: Decreases spindle speed override by 10%.

codes.name: Spindle Override +1%
codes.description: Increases spindle speed override by 1% for fine adjustment.

codes.name: Spindle Override -1%
codes.description: Decreases spindle speed override by 1% for fine adjustment.

codes.name: Toggle Spindle Stop
codes.description: Toggles the spindle enable/disable state, but only while in HOLD state. When motion resumes via cycle start, the last spindle state is restored with a 4-second delay before resuming the tool path. Ignored while in motion.

examples.description: Toggle spindle on/off during feed hold

crossReferences.note: Identical behavior in FluidNC. Only works in hold mode.

codes.notes: Real-time command. Only functional during HOLD state. Useful for inspecting the workpiece mid-job without losing position.

codes.name: Toggle Flood Coolant
codes.description: Toggles the flood coolant (M8/M9) output pin and directly changes the coolant modal state in the G-code parser. May be commanded in IDLE, RUN, or HOLD states.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Changes persist in the parser — subsequent M9 will turn off, M8 will turn on.

codes.name: Toggle Mist Coolant
codes.description: Toggles the mist coolant (M7/M9) output pin. Requires the ENABLE_M7 compile-time option. May be commanded in IDLE, RUN, or HOLD states.
codes.notes: Real-time command. Requires ENABLE_M7 compile-time option in grbl.

codes.name: Error 1: Expected Command Letter
codes.description: G-code words consist of a letter and a value. Letter was not found.

codes.name: Error 2: Bad Number Format
codes.description: Numeric value format is not valid or missing an expected value.

codes.name: Error 3: Invalid $ Command
codes.description: Grbl '$' system command was not recognized or supported.

codes.name: Error 4: Negative Value
codes.description: Negative value received for an expected positive value.

codes.name: Error 5: Homing Not Enabled
codes.description: Homing cycle is not enabled via settings ($22).

codes.name: Error 6: Step Pulse Too Short
codes.description: Minimum step pulse time must be greater than 3 microseconds.

codes.name: Error 7: EEPROM Read Failed
codes.description: EEPROM read failed. Settings have been reset and restored to default values.

crossReferences.note: Same error code in FluidNC (NVS instead of EEPROM).

codes.name: Error 8: Not Idle
codes.description: Grbl '$' command cannot be used unless grbl is IDLE. Ensures smooth operation during a job.

codes.name: Error 9: G-code Locked
codes.description: G-code locked out during alarm or jog state.

codes.name: Error 10: Soft Limits Need Homing
codes.description: Soft limits cannot be enabled without homing also enabled.

codes.name: Error 11: Line Too Long
codes.description: Max characters per line exceeded. Line was not processed and executed.

crossReferences.note: Same error code in grblHAL. grblHAL supports longer lines.

codes.notes: grbl has an 80-character line limit by default.

codes.name: Error 12: Max Step Rate Exceeded
codes.description: Grbl '$' setting value exceeds the maximum step rate supported. Compile-time option dependent.

codes.name: Error 13: Safety Door Opened
codes.description: Safety door detected as opened and door state initiated.

codes.name: Error 14: Startup Line Too Long
codes.description: Build info or startup line exceeded EEPROM line length limit. Grbl-Mega only.

codes.name: Error 15: Jog Exceeds Travel
codes.description: Jog target exceeds machine travel. Command ignored.

crossReferences.note: FluidNC constrains jogs to soft limits instead of throwing this error.

codes.name: Error 16: Invalid Jog Command
codes.description: Jog command with no '=' or contains prohibited G-code.

codes.name: Error 17: Laser Mode Requires PWM
codes.description: Laser mode requires PWM output. The spindle output pin must support PWM for laser mode ($32=1).

codes.name: Error 20: Unsupported G-code
codes.description: Unsupported or invalid G-code command found in block.

codes.name: Error 21: Modal Group Violation
codes.description: More than one G-code command from the same modal group found in block.

codes.name: Error 22: Undefined Feed Rate
codes.description: Feed rate has not yet been set or is undefined.

codes.name: Error 23: Integer Required
codes.description: G-code command in block requires an integer value.

codes.name: Error 24: Axis Command Conflict
codes.description: Two G-code commands that both require the use of the XYZ axis words were detected in the block.

codes.name: Error 25: Word Repeated
codes.description: A G-code word was repeated in the block.

codes.name: Error 26: No Axis Words
codes.description: A G-code command implicitly or explicitly requires XYZ axis words in the block, but none were detected.

codes.name: Error 27: Invalid Line Number
codes.description: N line number value is not within the valid range of 1 - 9,999,999.

codes.name: Error 28: Missing P or L Value
codes.description: A G-code command was sent, but is missing some required P or L value words in the line.

codes.name: Error 29: Unsupported Coordinate System
codes.description: Grbl supports six work coordinate systems G54-G59. G59.1, G59.2, and G59.3 are not supported.

crossReferences.note: grblHAL may support extended coordinate systems depending on build.

codes.name: Error 30: Invalid G53 Motion
codes.description: G53 requires either a G0 seek or G1 feed motion mode to be active. A different motion was active.

codes.name: Error 31: Unused Axis Words with G80
codes.description: There are unused axis words in the block and G80 motion mode cancel is active.

codes.name: Error 32: No Axis Words in Plane
codes.description: A G2 or G3 arc was commanded but there are no XYZ axis words in the selected plane to trace the arc.

codes.name: Error 33: Invalid Target
codes.description: The motion command has an invalid target. G2, G3, and G38.2 generate this error if the arc is impossible to generate or if the probe target is the current position.

codes.name: Error 34: Arc Radius Error
codes.description: A G2 or G3 arc, traced with the radius definition, had a mathematical error when computing the arc geometry. Try breaking up the arc into semi-circles or quadrants, or redefine with arc offset definition.

codes.name: Error 35: Missing Arc Offset
codes.description: A G2 or G3 arc, traced with the offset definition, is missing the IJK offset word in the selected plane to trace the arc.

codes.name: Error 36: Unused Words
codes.description: There are unused, leftover G-code words that aren't used by any command in the block.

codes.name: Error 37: Invalid G43.1 Axis
codes.description: The G43.1 dynamic tool length offset command cannot apply an offset to an axis other than its configured axis. The grbl default axis is the Z-axis.

codes.name: Error 38: Tool Number Exceeded
codes.description: Tool number greater than max supported value.

codes.name: Alarm 1: Hard Limit
codes.description: Hard limit triggered. Machine position is likely lost due to sudden and immediate halt. Re-homing is highly recommended.
codes.notes: Clear with Ctrl+X (soft reset), then $H to re-home. Position is unreliable after this alarm.

codes.name: Alarm 2: Soft Limit
codes.description: G-code motion target exceeds machine travel. Machine position is safely retained. Alarm may be safely unlocked.
codes.notes: Safe to unlock with $X — position was not lost. Check your G-code travel boundaries.

codes.name: Alarm 3: Abort During Cycle
codes.description: Reset while in motion. Grbl cannot guarantee position. Lost steps are likely. Re-homing is highly recommended.

codes.name: Alarm 4: Probe Fail (Initial State)
codes.description: Probe is not in the expected initial state before starting probe cycle. G38.2 and G38.3 require probe not triggered; G38.4 and G38.5 require probe triggered.

codes.name: Alarm 5: Probe Fail (No Contact)
codes.description: Probe did not contact the workpiece within the programmed travel for G38.2 and G38.4.

codes.name: Alarm 6: Homing Fail (Reset)
codes.description: Reset during active homing cycle.

codes.name: Alarm 7: Homing Fail (Door)
codes.description: Safety door was opened during active homing cycle.

codes.name: Alarm 8: Homing Fail (Pull-off)
codes.description: Cycle failed to clear limit switch when pulling off. Try increasing pull-off setting ($27) or check wiring.

codes.name: Alarm 9: Homing Fail (No Switch)
codes.description: Could not find limit switch within search distance. Defined as 1.5 × max_travel on search and 5 × pulloff on locate phases.

codes.name: Alarm 10: Homing Fail (Dual Axis)
codes.description: On dual axis machines, could not find the second limit switch for self-squaring.
